texts.label: text
texts.orig: ..'Membrane gas absorption utilises hollow fibre membranes in specially designed modules as contacting media for gas and liquid flows. General aspects of the process are discussed, followed by a description of an example application concerning the production of CO 2 for greenhouses. Finally, other areas of application of this new membrane based contacting equipment are presented, like NH 3 removal from process .. gas and high pressure applications such as natural gas treatment.
texts.text: ..'Membrane gas absorption utilises hollow fibre membranes in specially designed modules as contacting media for gas and liquid flows. General aspects of the process are discussed, followed by a description of an example application concerning the production of CO 2 for greenhouses. Finally, other areas of application of this new membrane based contacting equipment are presented, like NH 3 removal from process .. gas and high pressure applications such as natural gas treatment.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At present selective polymer membranes are commercially available for gas separation a~dpurification duties. The driving force for gas separation membranes is sustained by a preesuredifference across the membrane. The equipment is simple, small size and easy to operate. Current membrane selectivities and available driving forces limit the applicàtions of gas separation membranes to bulk removal of certain components (H2' C02~VOC, O 2 ) at relatively high concentration levels. For a very selective removal and removálat low concentration levels absorptlon processes are a more attractive choice. Membranegas absorption is a hybrid separation proces gas separation and purification technolQgyaiming to combine the advantages of membrane technology (large surface arE;a,compactness, flexibility) and absorption processes (selectivity) into a superior separationprocess.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The essential element in a membrane absorber is aporous polymer membrane. The g~s phase remains separated from the liquid absorbent as a result of the hydrophobicityof ! the membrane. A gas stream is fed along one side of the membrane. The components to be removed from the gas stream will diffuse through the gas filled pores of tlile membrane. On the other side of the membrane they will be absorbed into the absorption liquid. The use of a membrane absorber leads to the following advantages:
texts.text: The essential element in a membrane absorber is aporous polymer membrane. The g~s phase remains separated from the liquid absorbent as a result of the hydrophobicityof ! the membrane. A gas stream is fed along one side of the membrane. The components to be removed from the gas stream will diffuse through the gas filled pores of tlile membrane. On the other side of the membrane they will be absorbed into the absorption liquid. The use of a membrane absorber leads to the following advantages:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: -gas and liquid flow are independent resulting in avoidance of problems encountered in packed/tray columns such as flooding, foaming, channeling and entrainment,
texts.text: -gas and liquid flow are independent resulting in avoidance of problems encountered in packed/tray columns such as flooding, foaming, channeling and entrainment,

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: -the equipment becomes modular allowing for greater flexibility,
texts.text: -the equipment becomes modular allowing for greater flexibility,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The choice of a suitable combination of absorption liquid and membrane is very important. The hydrophobicity and porecharacteristics of the membrane material should be such that the pores remain gas-filled at an excess pressure of at least 0.5 bar and preferably more than 1 bar on the liquid side. For instance, a low surface tension absorption liquid (y< 60 mN/m) will in the course of time seep through porous polyclefin membranes having a poresizes of 0.2 urn. As a membrane material polytetrafluorethylene seems a better choice for low surface tension liquids because it is more hydrophobic and more inert. The required maximal pore size can be determined from the Laptace- Young equation:
texts.text: The choice of a suitable combination of absorption liquid and membrane is very important. The hydrophobicity and porecharacteristics of the membrane material should be such that the pores remain gas-filled at an excess pressure of at least 0.5 bar and preferably more than 1 bar on the liquid side. For instance, a low surface tension absorption liquid (y< 60 mN/m) will in the course of time seep through porous polyclefin membranes having a poresizes of 0.2 urn. As a membrane material polytetrafluorethylene seems a better choice for low surface tension liquids because it is more hydrophobic and more inert. The required maximal pore size can be determined from the Laptace- Young equation:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane absorber design cannot be based on conventional shell and tube membrane module designs. These designs have historically been based on the use of hollow fibre membranes for filtration duties with iII-defined flow conditions on the shell side of the membrane module. When membranes are used for contacting duties it is necessary that flow conditions are well-defined on either side of the membrane to achieve good mass transfer. Furthermore it is essential that, given the large volume flows of,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: for instance, flue gas streams, the module design is easy to scale up. TNO has patented (US No 5230796, EUR No 0509031) a membrane module design, shown in Figure 2, which has good mass transfer characteristics and good scale-up potential. This module design is being commercialised by XTO Membrane Technology, a joint venture between Netherlands-based membrane manufacturer X-Flow and TNO.

This module design is being commercialised by XTO Membrane Technology, a joint venture between Netherlands-based membrane manufacturer X-Flow and TNO.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the Netherlands carbon dioxide enrichment in greenhouses is widely practised. Increasing the carbon dioxide concentration in the greenhouse from 350 ppm to 500 ppm results in a production increaseof 25% for certain bulk crops. Carbon dioxide is currently generated by the combustion of natural gas which is wasteful as the resulting energy produced is not always utilised. The resulting flue gas mayalso contain impurities such as nitrous oxides and ethene which are detrimental to plant growth. Furthermore carbon dioxide requirement is high during summer when there is little or no energy requirement. The current process is therefore not an energy-efficient process. Carbon dioxide can also be produced in a cogeneration plant with an absorption process. The waste heat from the cogeneration plant is used to regenerate the absorption liquid. The carbon dioxide thus produced is of high purity and can be, used for enrichment without " detrimental consequences tor the plants. The combined supply of carbon dioxide and heat to greenhouses leads to an energy saving in excess of 30%. An initial cost estimate has resulted a carbon dioxide production cost of around 100 NLG (= 45 Euro)/tonne CO 2 . This makes it economically attractive to produce carbon dioxide by the membrane gas absorption process. Figure 3 shows the basic scheme for this applications.
texts.text: In the Netherlands carbon dioxide enrichment in greenhouses is widely practised. Increasing the carbon dioxide concentration in the greenhouse from 350 ppm to 500 ppm results in a production increaseof 25% for certain bulk crops. Carbon dioxide is currently generated by the combustion of natural gas which is wasteful as the resulting energy produced is not always utilised. The resulting flue gas mayalso contain impurities such as nitrous oxides and ethene which are detrimental to plant growth. Furthermore carbon dioxide requirement is high during summer when there is little or no energy requirement. The current process is therefore not an energy-efficient process. Carbon dioxide can also be produced in a cogeneration plant with an absorption process. The waste heat from the cogeneration plant is used to regenerate the absorption liquid. The carbon dioxide thus produced is of high purity and can be, used for enrichment without " detrimental consequences tor the plants. The combined supply of carbon dioxide and heat to greenhouses leads to an energy saving in excess of 30%. An initial cost estimate has resulted a carbon dioxide production cost of around 100 NLG (= 45 Euro)/tonne CO 2 . This makes it economically attractive to produce carbon dioxide by the membrane gas absorption process. Figure 3 shows the basic scheme for this applications.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the removal CO2 from f1uegases the usually employed monoethanolamine liquid is unsuitable for operation with polyolefin membrane due to its low surface tension and also because its corrosive and non-oxygen stability resulting in liquid waste streams. TNO has developed and patented a range of liquids, named CORAL (US Patent No 5,749,941), which show stabie operation in conjunction with these membranes. The performance of the membrane gas absorption system using CORAL liquids can be 'expressed as a mass transfer coefficient. Mass transfer coefficients for the CORAL liquids have been determined in a pilot plant at an industrial site under the relevant conditions. The pilot plant allows for investigation of the absorption process and the desorption process. The design specifications of the pilot plant are shown in Table 1.
texts.text: For the removal CO2 from f1uegases the usually employed monoethanolamine liquid is unsuitable for operation with polyolefin membrane due to its low surface tension and also because its corrosive and non-oxygen stability resulting in liquid waste streams. TNO has developed and patented a range of liquids, named CORAL (US Patent No 5,749,941), which show stabie operation in conjunction with these membranes. The performance of the membrane gas absorption system using CORAL liquids can be 'expressed as a mass transfer coefficient. Mass transfer coefficients for the CORAL liquids have been determined in a pilot plant at an industrial site under the relevant conditions. The pilot plant allows for investigation of the absorption process and the desorption process. The design specifications of the pilot plant are shown in Table 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A f1uegas is fed to a prototype transversal-f1ow membrane absorber (contactor). In this absorber the gas stream is CO 2 is transferred to a liquid stream in a countercurrent fashion. The rich liquid is fed to the top of a stripper via a plate heat exchanger. The stripper temperature is maintained at a temperature of around 120°C by an electric heating element. As a result of the temperature increase of the absorption liquid CO 2 is liberated". from the solution. Water is recovered in the condensor and trickles back into the stripper. The lean liquid exits the stripper at the bottom and is fed back to the membrane absorber via the heat exchanger and a storage vessel. The specifications of the prototype crossflow membrane module according designed to the TNO patent are shown in Table 2.
texts.text: A f1uegas is fed to a prototype transversal-f1ow membrane absorber (contactor). In this absorber the gas stream is CO 2 is transferred to a liquid stream in a countercurrent fashion. The rich liquid is fed to the top of a stripper via a plate heat exchanger. The stripper temperature is maintained at a temperature of around 120°C by an electric heating element. As a result of the temperature increase of the absorption liquid CO 2 is liberated". from the solution. Water is recovered in the condensor and trickles back into the stripper. The lean liquid exits the stripper at the bottom and is fed back to the membrane absorber via the heat exchanger and a storage vessel. The specifications of the prototype crossflow membrane module according designed to the TNO patent are shown in Table 2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The performance of a membrane gas absorption system based on monoethanolamine can be estimated from first principles as this system is weil documented (see e.g. [4]). For the CORAL liquids this exercise cannot yet be carried out. It is however possible to compare their performance of CORAL with a theoretica I estimate for an MEA-solution of identical composition. The results of this comparison are shown in Fig. 5 for solutions containing 2 M of CO 2 binding components. The mass transfer coefficient for 2 M MEA has been estimated at the mean conditions in the membrane absorber. Results of a number of experiments have been expressed as a mass transfer coefficient as a function of the liquid loading.
texts.text: The performance of a membrane gas absorption system based on monoethanolamine can be estimated from first principles as this system is weil documented (see e.g. [4]). For the CORAL liquids this exercise cannot yet be carried out. It is however possible to compare their performance of CORAL with a theoretica I estimate for an MEA-solution of identical composition. The results of this comparison are shown in Fig. 5 for solutions containing 2 M of CO 2 binding components. The mass transfer coefficient for 2 M MEA has been estimated at the mean conditions in the membrane absorber. Results of a number of experiments have been expressed as a mass transfer coefficient as a function of the liquid loading.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 5 Performance comparison between CORAL liquids and MEA at 2 M
texts.text: Figure 5 Performance comparison between CORAL liquids and MEA at 2 M

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CORAL20 appears to possess a slightly better mass transfer than 2 M MEA, whereas CORAL21 has superior mass transfer properties. From the point of view of mass transfer CORAL appears to be good alternative for MEA. This should result in additional volume reductions of the resulting equipment.
texts.text: CORAL20 appears to possess a slightly better mass transfer than 2 M MEA, whereas CORAL21 has superior mass transfer properties. From the point of view of mass transfer CORAL appears to be good alternative for MEA. This should result in additional volume reductions of the resulting equipment.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Furthermore, CORAL20 has shown a constant performance over a discontinuous testing· period of four months. During this period absorption/desorption experiments were carried out using the same amount of liquid. No loss in performance has been noticed despite the presence of oxygen. The liquid appears to be more robust and oxygen-resistant than monoethanolamine which suffers from degradation when exposed to air. Vapour losses of the active component can also be neglected as the CO 2 -absorbing components are of an ionic nature. Results from corrosion tests carried out at stripper conditions have revealed that the use of carbon stéel is quite adequate for all of the novel absorption liquids. This williead to a cost advantage over MEA systems.
texts.text: Furthermore, CORAL20 has shown a constant performance over a discontinuous testing· period of four months. During this period absorption/desorption experiments were carried out using the same amount of liquid. No loss in performance has been noticed despite the presence of oxygen. The liquid appears to be more robust and oxygen-resistant than monoethanolamine which suffers from degradation when exposed to air. Vapour losses of the active component can also be neglected as the CO 2 -absorbing components are of an ionic nature. Results from corrosion tests carried out at stripper conditions have revealed that the use of carbon stéel is quite adequate for all of the novel absorption liquids. This williead to a cost advantage over MEA systems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A development project has been carried out which was at the assessment of the economie feasibility of membrane gas absorption for carbon dioxide production for the horticultural industry. The project was primarily funded by the Netherlands agency for energy and the environment (NOVEM) but financial support has also been received from representatives from the energy industry, a plant manufacturer, a membrane manufacturer and XTO-Membrane Technology, a joint venture between TNO and X-Flow, a membrane manufacturer. XTO commercialises the TNO transversal-f1ow membrane module design for contacting duties.
